message.content:
Thoroughly explore this codebase to understand its architecture, build system, and structure. I need to create a CLAUDE.md file.

Focus on:
1. What kind of project is this? (ASP.NET MVC, Web API, etc.)
2. Project file (.csproj) - what framework, packages, build configuration
3. Solution file (.sln) if exists
4. Directory structure - Areas, Controllers, Models, Views
5. How is the project built? (MSBuild, dotnet CLI, Visual Studio)
6. Any test projects?
7. Configuration files (web.config, appsettings.json, etc.)
8. CSS/JS frameworks used
9. Database access patterns (Entity Framework, Dapper, ADO.NET?)
10. Authentication/authorization approach
11. Any README, .cursorrules, .github/copilot-instructions.md files
12. Key architectural patterns (dependency injection, repository pattern, etc.)
13. Important namespaces and how code is organized
14. The Areas pattern - what areas exist and what do they do
15. Any NuGet packages.config or PackageReference entries

Start by listing the root directory, then explore key files like .csproj, .sln, web.config, Global.asax, Startup.cs, etc.

     5→## Getting started
     6→
     7→To make it easy for you to get started with GitLab, here's a list of recommended next steps.
     8→
     9→Already a pro? Just edit this README.md and make it your own. Want to make it easy? [Use the template at the bottom](#editing-this-readme)!
    10→
    11→## Add your files
    12→
    13→- [ ] [Create](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/repository/web_editor.html#create-a-file) or [upload](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/repository/web_editor.html#upload-a-file) files
    14→- [ ] [Add files using the command line](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/gitlab-basics/add-file.html#add-a-file-using-the-command-line) or push an existing Git repository with the following command:
    15→
    16→```
    17→cd existing_repo
    18→git remote add origin https://gitlab.lcx.app/lcx-le/application/befirst/portal.git
    19→git branch -M main
    20→git push -uf origin main
    21→```
    22→
    23→## Integrate with your tools
    24→
    25→- [ ] [Set up project integrations](https://gitlab.lcx.app/lcx-le/application/befirst/portal/-/settings/integrations)
    26→
    27→## Collaborate with your team
    28→
    29→- [ ] [Invite team members and collaborators](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/members/)
    30→- [ ] [Create a new merge request](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/merge_requests/creating_merge_requests.html)
    31→- [ ] [Automatically close issues from merge requests](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/issues/managing_issues.html#closing-issues-automatically)
    32→- [ ] [Enable merge request approvals](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/merge_requests/approvals/)
    33→- [ ] [Set auto-merge](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/merge_requests/merge_when_pipeline_succeeds.html)
    34→
    35→## Test and Deploy
    36→
    37→Use the built-in continuous integration in GitLab.
    38→
    39→- [ ] [Get started with GitLab CI/CD](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/ci/quick_start/index.html)
    40→- [ ] [Analyze your code for known vulnerabilities with Static Application Security Testing (SAST)](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/application_security/sast/)
    41→- [ ] [Deploy to Kubernetes, Amazon EC2, or Amazon ECS using Auto Deploy](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/topics/autodevops/requirements.html)
    42→- [ ] [Use pull-based deployments for improved Kubernetes management](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/clusters/agent/)
    43→- [ ] [Set up protected environments](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/ci/environments/protected_environments.html)
    44→
    45→***
    46→
    47→# Editing this README
    48→
    49→When you're ready to make this README your own, just edit this file and use the handy template below (or feel free to structure it however you want - this is just a starting point!). Thanks to [makeareadme.com](https://www.makeareadme.com/) for this template.
    50→
    51→## Suggestions for a good README
    52→
    53→Every project is different, so consider which of these sections apply to yours. The sections used in the template are suggestions for most open source projects. Also keep in mind that while a README can be too long and detailed, too long is better than too short. If you think your README is too long, consider utilizing another form of documentation rather than cutting out information.
    54→
    55→## Name
    56→Choose a self-explaining name for your project.
    57→
    58→## Description
    59→Let people know what your project can do specifically. Provide context and add a link to any reference visitors might be unfamiliar with. A list of Features or a Background subsection can also be added here. If there are alternatives to your project, this is a good place to list differentiating factors.
    60→
    61→## Badges
    62→On some READMEs, you may see small images that convey metadata, such as whether or not all the tests are passing for the project. You can use Shields to add some to your README. Many services also have instructions for adding a badge.
    63→
    64→## Visuals
    65→Depending on what you are making, it can be a good idea to include screenshots or even a video (you'll frequently see GIFs rather than actual videos). Tools like ttygif can help, but check out Asciinema for a more sophisticated method.
    66→
    67→## Installation
    68→Within a particular ecosystem, there may be a common way of installing things, such as using Yarn, NuGet, or Homebrew. However, consider the possibility that whoever is reading your README is a novice and would like more guidance. Listing specific steps helps remove ambiguity and gets people to using your project as quickly as possible. If it only runs in a specific context like a particular programming language version or operating system or has dependencies that have to be installed manually, also add a Requirements subsection.
    69→
    70→## Usage
    71→Use examples liberally, and show the expected output if you can. It's helpful to have inline the smallest example of usage that you can demonstrate, while providing links to more sophisticated examples if they are too long to reasonably include in the README.
    72→
    73→## Support
    74→Tell people where they can go to for help. It can be any combination of an issue tracker, a chat room, an email address, etc.
    75→
    76→## Roadmap
    77→If you have ideas for releases in the future, it is a good idea to list them in the README.
    78→
    79→## Contributing
    80→State if you are open to contributions and what your requirements are for accepting them.
    81→
    82→For people who want to make changes to your project, it's helpful to have some documentation on how to get started. Perhaps there is a script that they should run or some environment variables that they need to set. Make these steps explicit. These instructions could also be useful to your future self.
    83→
    84→You can also document commands to lint the code or run tests. These steps help to ensure high code quality and reduce the likelihood that the changes inadvertently break something. Having instructions for running tests is especially helpful if it requires external setup, such as starting a Selenium server for testing in a browser.
    85→
    86→## Authors and acknowledgment
    87→Show your appreciation to those who have contributed to the project.
    88→
    89→## License
    90→For open source projects, say how it is licensed.
    91→
    92→## Project status
    93→If you have run out of energy or time for your project, put a note at the top of the README saying that development has slowed down or stopped completely. Someone may choose to fork your project or volunteer to step in as a maintainer or owner, allowing your project to keep going. You can also make an explicit request for maintainers.
    94→

---

## BeFirst Portal - Codebase Architecture Analysis

### Project Overview

**Project Type**: ASP.NET MVC 5 Web Application (Classic .NET Framework)

**Solution**: Portal_BeFirst.sln  
**Main Project**: Portal_BeFirst.csproj (located in `S:\01_Repos\BeFirst_Portal\firstronic\`)  
**Assembly Name**: Firstronic  
**Root Namespace**: Firstronic  
**Target Framework**: .NET Framework 4.8  
**Project GUID**: {50C8775E-C4C1-4C97-B29A-476D2A11B501}

### 1. Project Structure

#### Root Directory Layout
```
S:\01_Repos\BeFirst_Portal\
├── firstronic/                    # Main project directory
│   ├── App_Start/                 # Application startup configuration
│   ├── Areas/                     # MVC Areas (18 functional areas)
│   ├── Controllers/               # Root-level controllers
│   ├── Models/                    # View models and domain models
│   ├── Views/                     # Razor views
│   ├── Framework/                 # Custom framework/infrastructure code
│   ├── Content/                   # Static assets (CSS, JS, images)
│   ├── Scripts/                   # JavaScript files
│   ├── Properties/                # Assembly info
│   ├── ExcelUploads/              # File upload storage
│   ├── UploadedFiles/             # File upload storage
│   ├── Global.asax.cs             # Application entry point
│   ├── Startup.cs                 # OWIN startup
│   ├── Web.config                 # Configuration
│   └── Portal_BeFirst.csproj      # Project file (6,787 lines)
├── packages/                      # NuGet packages (local cache)
├── Portal_BeFirst.sln             # Solution file
├── package.json                   # NPM dependencies (TypeScript, rollup, three.js)
└── README.md                      # Basic Vue.js template README (outdated)
```

### 2. MVC Areas Pattern (18 Areas)

The application uses **ASP.NET MVC Areas** to organize functionality by department/module:

| Area | Purpose | Example Controllers |
|------|---------|-------------------|
| **Dashboards** | Production monitoring dashboards, TV displays | DowntimeController, TVsController, FloorShopController, AreasController |
| **Engineering** | Engineering processes | AssemblyInstructionsController |
| **IS** | Information Systems | - |
| **IT** | IT management | - |
| **Manage** | System management | MachinesService, PLEX data |
| **Manufacturing** | Manufacturing operations | - |
| **Materials** | Material management | - |
| **Mobile** | Mobile interfaces | - |
| **Personnel** | HR/employee management | - |
| **Planning** | Production planning | - |
| **Production** | Production tracking | ShortagesController, GoalDailyController, ReportsController, UnitsController |
| **Purchasing** | Procurement | - |
| **Quality** | Quality control | (Deleted: BackTrackController, ContinualImprovementController) |
| **Rework** | Rework tracking | - |
| **Testing** | Testing operations | - |
| **Tickets** | Ticketing system | Dashboard, Reports |
| **Tracking** | Unit tracking | UnitsController, TrackingController, SetupCheckController |
| **Utils** | Utilities | PrinterController |

**Total Controllers**: 386 controller files across all areas

Each Area has:
- `{Area}AreaRegistration.cs` - Route registration
- `Controllers/` - Area-specific controllers
- `Models/` - Area-specific models
- `Views/` - Area-specific Razor views
- `_ViewStart.cshtml` - Layout configuration

### 3. Framework Layer (`firstronic\Framework\`)

Custom framework providing infrastructure services:

```
Framework/
├── Account/              # Identity/authentication (ApplicationUser, ApplicationUserService)
├── APIs/                 # API integrations (Station, Materials, Containers, Account)
├── Attributes/           # Custom MVC attributes
├── Cache/                # Memory caching (MemoryCacheManager, ICacheManager)
├── Charts/               # Charting components
├── Class/                # Shared classes
├── Core/                 # Core infrastructure (Engine - DI container wrapper)
├── Data/                 # Data access layer
│   ├── AppDataContext/   # New EF Core context (AppDbContext)
│   ├── LegacyDataContext/# Legacy EF6 context (DataContext)
│   ├── Repository.cs     # Generic repository pattern
│   └── IRepository.cs    # Repository interface
├── Emails/               # Email services
├── Entities/             # Base entities (BaseEntity, Helpers)
├── Error/                # Error handling (Logs)
├── Extensions/           # Extension methods
├── Functions/            # Utility functions (Queries - raw SQL helpers)
├── GlobalVariables/      # Configuration (connection strings from env vars)
├── Helpers/              # Helper classes
├── Infrastructure/       # MVC infrastructure (WebViewPage, GenericRoute)
├── Languages/            # Localization (Settings.resx, LanguageMang)
├── ModelBase/            # Base model classes
├── Paging/               # Pagination helpers
├── Plex/                 # PLEX ERP integration
├── Queries/              # SQL query helpers
├── Services/             # Business services
├── Strings/              # String utilities
├── UI/                   # UI components (ViewComponentBase, custom HTML helpers)
├── Upload/               # File upload handling
├── BaseController.cs     # Base controller with notifications, language support
├── BePlex.cs             # PLEX integration (480KB file - major integration)
└── BeSecurity.cs         # Security helpers
```

### 4. Build System & Configuration

#### Build Tools
- **Build System**: MSBuild via Visual Studio 2015+ (ToolsVersion="15.0")
- **Project Type**: ASP.NET Web Application (ProjectTypeGuids: {349c5851-65df-11da-9384-00065b846f21})
- **Compilation**: Debug and Release configurations
- **TypeScript**: Microsoft.TypeScript.MSBuild 4.8.4
- **NPM**: Used for TypeScript compilation, rollup bundling

#### Startup Configuration (`App_Start/`)
1. **RouteConfig.cs** - MVC routing (default: Home/Index)
2. **BundleConfig.cs** - CSS/JS bundling (Bootstrap, jQuery, Font Awesome)
3. **FilterConfig.cs** - Global action filters
4. **DependencyRegister.cs** - Autofac IoC container configuration
5. **Startup.Auth.cs** - OWIN authentication middleware

#### Application Startup Flow
```
Global.asax.cs → Application_Start()
  ↓
  1. ViewEngines.Engines.Add(RazorViewEngine)
  2. AreaRegistration.RegisterAllAreas()
  3. RouteConfig.RegisterRoutes()
  4. BundleConfig.RegisterBundles()
  5. DependencyRegister.Register() → Autofac container setup
  6. MvcHandler.DisableMvcResponseHeader = true (security)

Startup.cs → Configuration(IAppBuilder app)
  ↓
  ConfigureAuth(app) → Cookie authentication, CSP headers
```

### 5. Key NuGet Packages & Dependencies

#### Core Framework
- **ASP.NET MVC**: 5.2.7
- **Entity Framework**: 6.4.4 (legacy context)
- **Entity Framework Core**: 3.1.32 (new context)
- **Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc**: 2.1.3 (mixed with classic MVC)

#### Dependency Injection
- **Autofac**: 5.1.2
- **Autofac.Mvc5**: 5.0.0

#### Authentication & Identity
- **Microsoft.AspNet.Identity.Core**: 2.2.4
- **Microsoft.AspNet.Identity.EntityFramework**: 2.2.3
- **Microsoft.AspNet.Identity.Owin**: 2.2.4
- **Microsoft.Owin**: Cookie authentication

#### Data Access
- **Microsoft.Data.SqlClient**: 6.0.1 (modern SQL Server client)
- **Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.SqlServer**: 3.1.32
- **Dapper** (not explicitly listed, but SQL queries via Framework.Queries)

#### Excel & Document Processing
- **EPPlus**: 7.2.2 (Excel generation)
- **ClosedXML**: 0.95.4 (Excel manipulation)
- **ExcelDataReader**: 3.6.0 (Excel reading)
- **DocumentFormat.OpenXml**: 2.7.2

#### Barcode & PDF
- **IronBarCode**: 2024.8.0.12
- **IronSoftware**: Various packages for image/barcode processing

#### Frontend Libraries (Content/vendor/)
- **Bootstrap**: 5.3.3
- **jQuery**: 3.7.1
- **Font Awesome**: 6.x
- **Chart.js**: Charting library
- **TinyMCE**: Rich text editor
- **Select2**: Enhanced select boxes
- **DataTables** (mvc-grid)
- **Three.js**: 3D visualization (for FloorShop 3D layout)
- **D3.js**: Data visualization
- **Toastr**: Notifications
